content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_4929293a

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write; transcript includes stable personal event, wedding preferences, planning status, and richer context about inspiration and desired ceremony atmosphere.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_4929293a:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user wants the wedding planner's input on the vendor selection process, especially because the user is still deciding between the countryside and city wedding venues.
- The user is planning a small ceremony for the user's own wedding next year.
- The user is deciding between a cozy countryside bed and breakfast and a beautiful city art museum as the user's wedding venue.
- The user is considering hiring a wedding planner to help with the logistics for the user's wedding.
- The user plans to schedule a consultation with a wedding planner to better understand how the planner can help with wedding logistics.
- note hot-path-092a52299dab-note-2: CONTEXT; Wedding venue tradeoff: countryside B&B vs city art museum; For the user's small wedding planned for next year, the two active venue options are a cozy countryside bed and breakfast and a beautiful city art museum. The B&B is framed as more intimate, rustic, private, and potentially convenient for on-site lodging, while the museum is framed as more elegant, flexible, amenity-rich, accessible, and potentially more formal or expensive.; scope=Applies to the user's wedding venue decision between the countryside bed and breakfast and the city art museum.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-8: has_goal; The user is deciding between a cozy countryside bed and breakfast and a beautiful city art museum as the user's wedding venue.; scope=User's wedding venue decision in the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- note hot-path-092a52299dab-note-3: PLAN; Planner consultation intended to inform logistics and vendor selection; The user plans to schedule a wedding planner consultation while still deciding between the two venues, with the goal of understanding logistics and getting the planner's input on vendor selection. The vendor question should specifically cover countryside versus city vendor experience and how location affects planning complexity.; scope=Applies to the user's wedding planner consultation and vendor-selection approach for the wedding.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-6: has_goal; The user is planning a small ceremony for the user's own wedding next year.; scope=User's wedding planning discussed in the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- note hot-path-092a52299dab-note-5: CONTEXT; Michael's rooftop engagement party influenced wedding planning context; The user attended Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar on the same day as this session, and that event prompted reflection on the user's own wedding plans. This is useful context for why rooftop/bar-style or urban venue inspiration may arise in later wedding discussions.; scope=Applies to the user's wedding planning inspiration from Michael's engagement party.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-11: has_goal; The user wants the wedding planner's input on the vendor selection process, especially because the user is still deciding between the countryside and city wedding venues.; scope=User's wedding vendor-selection planning in the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-10: has_goal; The user plans to schedule a consultation with a wedding planner to better understand how the planner can help with wedding logistics.; scope=User's wedding planner consultation plan in the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-12: believes; The example wedding planner says countryside vendors may be more limited in selection and availability but often provide a more personalized, intimate, bespoke experience.; scope=Imported assistant-generated example planner response in the 2023-05-06 session; useful as generated planning context, not a real planner's verified statement.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-9: has_goal; The user is considering hiring a wedding planner to help with the logistics for the user's wedding.; scope=User's wedding logistics planning in the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-14: believes; The example wedding planner recommends starting the vendor search earlier for a countryside wedding venue and staying flexible about availability and logistics.; scope=Imported assistant-generated example planner response in the 2023-05-06 session; useful as generated planning context, not a real planner's verified statement.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-20: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response listed Blooming Beauty as a city florist that can create modern structural arrangements and lush romantic bouquets for large events.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.
- note hot-path-092a52299dab-note-4: CONTEXT; Example countryside and city wedding vendor shortlist; The planner example distinguishes countryside vendors from city vendors: Green Meadow Farm Catering for farm-to-table countryside catering, Emily May Photography for rustic countryside photography, Petal & Twig for local-farm whimsical florals, City Bites Catering for city catering and large groups, Urban Lens Photography for sleek urban photography, and Blooming Beauty for city florals ranging from structural arrangements to romantic bouquets.; scope=Applies as an imported example vendor shortlist for the user's wedding planning comparison between countryside and city venues.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-7: attended_event; The user attended Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar on 2023-05-06.; scope=User's dated social event attendance from the 2023-05-06 imported session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-16: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response listed Emily May Photography as a countryside photographer with a rustic style suited to countryside weddings.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-17: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response listed Petal & Twig as a countryside florist that sources flowers from local farms and creates whimsical arrangements suited to countryside weddings.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-19: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response listed Urban Lens Photography as a city photographer with a sleek, modern style suited to city weddings.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-13: believes; The example wedding planner says city vendors tend to be more abundant and offer more options, but they may be more expensive and have stricter logistical requirements.; scope=Imported assistant-generated example planner response in the 2023-05-06 session; useful as generated planning context, not a real planner's verified statement.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-18: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response recommended City Bites Catering as a city caterer offering a range of cuisines and able to accommodate large groups.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.
- claim hot-path-092a52299dab-claim-15: generated_artifact_detail; The generated example planner response recommended Green Meadow Farm Catering as a countryside caterer offering farm-to-table cuisine and accommodating a wide range of dietary needs.; scope=Imported assistant-generated vendor recommendation list from the 2023-05-06 session.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my / I'm / we / our; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I / my / I'm / we / our; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=my own wedding / my own big day / my wedding / our wedding; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<1>; type=existing; name=my own wedding / my own big day / my wedding / our wedding; reason=Mentions refer to the user's wedding, which already exists as a candidate entity.
- mention=wedding planner / full-service planner / planner / coordinator / day-of coordinator / wedding consultant / venue coordinator; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<2>; type=existing; name=wedding planner / full-service planner / planner / coordinator / day-of coordinator / wedding consultant / venue coordinator; reason=Mentions refer broadly to the wedding planning/coordinator role already represented by the Wedding planner concept.
- mention=cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside / countryside bed and breakfast / bed and breakfast; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<3>; type=existing; name=cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside / countryside bed and breakfast / bed and breakfast; reason=This is the existing candidate location for the countryside B&B wedding venue.
- mention=beautiful art museum in the city / city art museum / art museum; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<4>; type=existing; name=beautiful art museum in the city / city art museum / art museum; reason=This is the existing candidate location for the city art museum wedding venue.

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/06/15 (Thu) 17:40
Transcript:
user: I'm getting ready to start planning my own wedding and I was wondering if you could give me some advice on how to choose the right wedding planner. By the way, I just walked down the aisle as a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding today, and it got me thinking about my own big day.
assistant: Congratulations on being a part of your cousin's special day! It's wonderful that it's got you thinking about your own wedding plans.

Choosing the right wedding planner can make a huge difference in the success and stress-level of your big day. Here are some tips to help you find the perfect planner for you:

1. **Define your needs**: Before searching for a planner, consider what kind of support you need. Do you want a full-service planner, a day-of coordinator, or a consultant? Do you have a specific style or theme in mind? Knowing what you need will help you find a planner who specializes in that area.
2. **Get referrals**: Ask friends, family, or recently married couples for recommendations. They can provide valuable insights into a planner's work ethic, communication style, and overall performance.
3. **Research online**: Look for planners in your area and check their:
	* Website: Is it professional, easy to navigate, and showcase their work?
	* Social media: Do they share behind-the-scenes content, client testimonials, and industry insights?
	* Reviews: Check online review platforms like WeddingWire, The Knot, or Google Reviews to see what past clients have to say.
4. **Check credentials**: Ensure the planner is certified by a reputable organization, such as the Association of Bridal Consultants (ABC) or the Wedding Planning Institute (WPI). This indicates they have received proper training and adhere to industry standards.
5. **Meet with potential planners**: Schedule consultations with 2-3 planners to discuss your vision, budget, and expectations. Pay attention to:
	* Chemistry: Do you feel comfortable and connected with the planner?
	* Communication: Are they responsive, clear, and concise in their communication?
	* Experience: Have they worked with couples with similar needs and budgets?
6. **Ask the right questions**: During the consultation, ask questions like:
	* What's your approach to wedding planning, and how will you ensure my vision is executed?
	* How will you handle last-minute changes or unexpected issues on the wedding day?
	* What's your pricing structure, and what's included in your services?
	* Can you provide a detailed timeline and checklist for the planning process?
7. **Check their vendor relationships**: A good planner should have established relationships with reputable vendors. Ask about their preferred vendor lists and how they'll help you find the right vendors for your needs.
8. **Trust your instincts**: Ultimately, choose a planner who makes you feel confident, supported, and excited about the planning process.

Remember, your wedding planner will be your right-hand person during the planning process, so it's essential to find someone who understands your vision, budget, and needs.

Best of luck with your wedding planning, and I hope these tips help you find the perfect planner for your special day!
user: I'll definitely consider these tips. I'm actually thinking of having a small, intimate ceremony, possibly at a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you think a wedding planner would be necessary for a small wedding like that?
assistant: A small, intimate ceremony sounds lovely! While a wedding planner can be beneficial for any size wedding, it ultimately depends on your personal preferences, budget, and the complexity of your plans.

For a small, low-key wedding like the ones you described, you might not need a full-service wedding planner. However, consider the following scenarios where a planner or coordinator could still be helpful:

1. **Logistics and coordination**: Even with a small guest list, you'll still need to manage venue logistics, catering, decor, and timings. A planner or coordinator can help with these details, ensuring everything runs smoothly on the day.
2. **Vendor management**: If you're hiring vendors like photographers, florists, or musicians, a planner can help you find the right ones, negotiate contracts, and coordinate their services.
3. **Timeline creation and management**: A planner can create a detailed timeline for the day, ensuring that everything happens according to plan and that you can relax and enjoy the celebration.
4. **Day-of coordination**: Even if you've planned most of the details yourself, having a professional on-site to oversee the setup, coordinate vendors, and troubleshoot any issues can be invaluable.

If you decide you don't need a full-service planner, you might consider hiring a:

1. **Day-of coordinator**: They'll take care of the logistics and ensure everything runs smoothly on the wedding day.
2. **Wedding consultant**: They'll provide guidance and advice on specific aspects of the planning process, like vendor selection or timeline creation.

For a small, intimate wedding, you might also consider the following alternatives to a traditional wedding planner:

1. **Venue coordinator**: Many bed and breakfasts or art museums have in-house coordinators who can help with logistics and vendor management.
2. **Friend or family member**: Ask a trusted friend or family member to act as a coordinator or point person on the wedding day. Just be sure to give them clear instructions and responsibilities.

Ultimately, it's up to you to decide whether a wedding planner or coordinator is necessary for your small, intimate ceremony. If you're comfortable with the planning process and have a clear vision, you might be able to manage the details yourself. However, if you want to enjoy the celebration and leave the logistics to a professional, consider hiring a planner or coordinator to help make your special day truly unforgettable.
user: I'm thinking of having a small, intimate ceremony, possibly at a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you think I could pull off a fall wedding without a wedding planner, considering I've already started looking at venues and have a pretty clear idea of what I want?
assistant: A fall wedding sounds lovely! With a clear idea of what you want and having already started looking at venues, you're off to a great start. Pulling off a small, intimate fall wedding without a wedding planner is definitely possible, especially if you're organized and willing to put in the effort.

Here are some pros and cons to consider:

**Pros:**

1. **Cost savings**: Not hiring a wedding planner can save you money, which you can allocate to other aspects of the wedding.
2. **Personal touch**: By handling the planning yourself, you can ensure that every detail reflects your personal style and vision.
3. **Flexibility**: Without a planner, you can make changes or adjustments as you see fit, without having to consult with someone else.

**Cons:**

1. **Time and effort**: Planning a wedding, even a small one, requires a significant amount of time and energy. Be prepared to dedicate several hours a week to planning and coordination.
2. **Stress and overwhelm**: Without a planner to handle logistics and troubleshooting, you may feel overwhelmed or stressed if something goes wrong.
3. **Lack of expertise**: While you may have a clear idea of what you want, a professional planner has experience and knowledge of the industry, which can be invaluable in avoiding mistakes or finding creative solutions.

To increase your chances of success, consider the following tips:

1. **Create a detailed timeline**: Break down the planning process into manageable tasks and deadlines to stay on track.
2. **Make a budget and stick to it**: Establish a budget and prioritize your spending to ensure you allocate resources effectively.
3. **Communicate with vendors**: Clearly communicate your vision and expectations to vendors, and be prepared to negotiate contracts and details.
4. **Recruit help**: Ask friends or family members to assist with specific tasks, like decorating or coordinating the rehearsal.
5. **Be flexible**: Be prepared for unexpected issues and have a plan B (or C, or D!) in case something goes wrong.

If you're comfortable with the potential challenges and are willing to put in the effort, you can definitely pull off a beautiful fall wedding without a wedding planner. Just remember to take care of yourself during the planning process and enjoy the journey!

What's your favorite part about planning your wedding so far?
user: I'm really enjoying looking at different venues and imagining how I can make the space feel intimate and special. Walking down the aisle as a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding got me thinking about how I want to create a similar atmosphere at my own wedding.
assistant: That's wonderful! It's great that you're drawing inspiration from your cousin's wedding and thinking about how to create a unique and intimate atmosphere at your own wedding.

Venues can really set the tone for the entire wedding, and it sounds like you're taking the time to find the perfect one for your special day. Considering the details that will make the space feel intimate and special is a great approach.

As you continue to explore venues, you might want to ask yourself some questions to help you visualize and create the atmosphere you want:

* What are the must-haves for your wedding ceremony and reception? (e.g., natural light, outdoor space, specific decor, etc.)
* How do you want your guests to feel when they arrive at the venue? (e.g., welcome, relaxed, excited, etc.)
* What kind of ambiance do you want to create with lighting, music, and decor?
* Are there any personal touches or meaningful elements you want to incorporate into the space?

By thinking through these questions, you'll be able to find a venue that fits your vision and create an atmosphere that's truly reflective of your personal style and love story.

It's also wonderful that you got to experience being a bridesmaid at your cousin's wedding. That can be such a special and inspiring experience! Did you learn anything from that experience that you're applying to your own wedding planning process?
user: Yeah, being a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding really made me think about the little details that can make a big difference. Like, I loved how she had a special song played during the ceremony that was meaningful to her and her partner. It was such a sweet touch that really made the moment feel more intimate. I'm definitely going to incorporate something similar into my own wedding.
assistant: That's so sweet! It's great that you're taking inspiration from your cousin's wedding and thinking about how to make your own ceremony even more personal and meaningful.

Those little details can really make a big difference in creating an unforgettable experience for you, your partner, and your guests. A special song during the ceremony can be a beautiful way to add an emotional touch and make the moment feel more intimate.

Incorporating personal elements like that can also help to make your wedding feel more authentic and reflective of your relationship. It's a great way to tell your love story and share it with your guests.

What kind of special details are you thinking of incorporating into your own ceremony or reception? Are there any specific songs, rituals, or traditions that hold meaning for you and your partner?

Remember, it's all about making your wedding day feel true to who you are as a couple, and those little details can really help to make it feel more special and memorable.
user: I've been thinking about having a special reading or poem during the ceremony that speaks to our love story. We've also been talking about doing a unity candle or sand ceremony to symbolize our commitment to each other. And, of course, we want to make sure our vows are personal and meaningful to us. We're still brainstorming, but we want to make sure our ceremony feels intimate and romantic.
assistant: I love those ideas! A special reading or poem can be a beautiful way to add an emotional depth to the ceremony and make it feel more personal. It's a great way to express your feelings and tell your love story in a unique and meaningful way.

The unity candle or sand ceremony is also a wonderful idea! Those types of rituals can be so powerful in symbolizing your commitment to each other and creating a sense of unity. It's a beautiful way to visualize your love and commitment to each other, and it can be a really special moment to share with your guests.

And, of course, personal and meaningful vows are a must! Writing your own vows can be a really romantic and intimate way to express your love and commitment to each other. It's a great way to make the ceremony feel more personal and special, and it's something that you'll both treasure for years to come.

It sounds like you're on the right track in terms of creating an intimate and romantic ceremony. Remember to take your time, be creative, and don't be afraid to think outside the box. Your ceremony should reflect your personalities, your love story, and your commitment to each other.

What's been the most fun part of planning your ceremony so far? Have you found any inspiration in unexpected places, or have any surprises come up during the planning process?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed

Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_4929293a

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write; transcript includes stable personal event, wedding preferences, planning status, and richer context about inspiration and desired ceremony atmosphere.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/06/15 (Thu) 17:40
Transcript:
user: I'm getting ready to start planning my own wedding and I was wondering if you could give me some advice on how to choose the right wedding planner. By the way, I just walked down the aisle as a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding today, and it got me thinking about my own big day.
assistant: Congratulations on being a part of your cousin's special day! It's wonderful that it's got you thinking about your own wedding plans.

Choosing the right wedding planner can make a huge difference in the success and stress-level of your big day. Here are some tips to help you find the perfect planner for you:

1. **Define your needs**: Before searching for a planner, consider what kind of support you need. Do you want a full-service planner, a day-of coordinator, or a consultant? Do you have a specific style or theme in mind? Knowing what you need will help you find a planner who specializes in that area.
2. **Get referrals**: Ask friends, family, or recently married couples for recommendations. They can provide valuable insights into a planner's work ethic, communication style, and overall performance.
3. **Research online**: Look for planners in your area and check their:
	* Website: Is it professional, easy to navigate, and showcase their work?
	* Social media: Do they share behind-the-scenes content, client testimonials, and industry insights?
	* Reviews: Check online review platforms like WeddingWire, The Knot, or Google Reviews to see what past clients have to say.
4. **Check credentials**: Ensure the planner is certified by a reputable organization, such as the Association of Bridal Consultants (ABC) or the Wedding Planning Institute (WPI). This indicates they have received proper training and adhere to industry standards.
5. **Meet with potential planners**: Schedule consultations with 2-3 planners to discuss your vision, budget, and expectations. Pay attention to:
	* Chemistry: Do you feel comfortable and connected with the planner?
	* Communication: Are they responsive, clear, and concise in their communication?
	* Experience: Have they worked with couples with similar needs and budgets?
6. **Ask the right questions**: During the consultation, ask questions like:
	* What's your approach to wedding planning, and how will you ensure my vision is executed?
	* How will you handle last-minute changes or unexpected issues on the wedding day?
	* What's your pricing structure, and what's included in your services?
	* Can you provide a detailed timeline and checklist for the planning process?
7. **Check their vendor relationships**: A good planner should have established relationships with reputable vendors. Ask about their preferred vendor lists and how they'll help you find the right vendors for your needs.
8. **Trust your instincts**: Ultimately, choose a planner who makes you feel confident, supported, and excited about the planning process.

Remember, your wedding planner will be your right-hand person during the planning process, so it's essential to find someone who understands your vision, budget, and needs.

Best of luck with your wedding planning, and I hope these tips help you find the perfect planner for your special day!
user: I'll definitely consider these tips. I'm actually thinking of having a small, intimate ceremony, possibly at a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you think a wedding planner would be necessary for a small wedding like that?
assistant: A small, intimate ceremony sounds lovely! While a wedding planner can be beneficial for any size wedding, it ultimately depends on your personal preferences, budget, and the complexity of your plans.

For a small, low-key wedding like the ones you described, you might not need a full-service wedding planner. However, consider the following scenarios where a planner or coordinator could still be helpful:

1. **Logistics and coordination**: Even with a small guest list, you'll still need to manage venue logistics, catering, decor, and timings. A planner or coordinator can help with these details, ensuring everything runs smoothly on the day.
2. **Vendor management**: If you're hiring vendors like photographers, florists, or musicians, a planner can help you find the right ones, negotiate contracts, and coordinate their services.
3. **Timeline creation and management**: A planner can create a detailed timeline for the day, ensuring that everything happens according to plan and that you can relax and enjoy the celebration.
4. **Day-of coordination**: Even if you've planned most of the details yourself, having a professional on-site to oversee the setup, coordinate vendors, and troubleshoot any issues can be invaluable.

If you decide you don't need a full-service planner, you might consider hiring a:

1. **Day-of coordinator**: They'll take care of the logistics and ensure everything runs smoothly on the wedding day.
2. **Wedding consultant**: They'll provide guidance and advice on specific aspects of the planning process, like vendor selection or timeline creation.

For a small, intimate wedding, you might also consider the following alternatives to a traditional wedding planner:

1. **Venue coordinator**: Many bed and breakfasts or art museums have in-house coordinators who can help with logistics and vendor management.
2. **Friend or family member**: Ask a trusted friend or family member to act as a coordinator or point person on the wedding day. Just be sure to give them clear instructions and responsibilities.

Ultimately, it's up to you to decide whether a wedding planner or coordinator is necessary for your small, intimate ceremony. If you're comfortable with the planning process and have a clear vision, you might be able to manage the details yourself. However, if you want to enjoy the celebration and leave the logistics to a professional, consider hiring a planner or coordinator to help make your special day truly unforgettable.
user: I'm thinking of having a small, intimate ceremony, possibly at a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you think I could pull off a fall wedding without a wedding planner, considering I've already started looking at venues and have a pretty clear idea of what I want?
assistant: A fall wedding sounds lovely! With a clear idea of what you want and having already started looking at venues, you're off to a great start. Pulling off a small, intimate fall wedding without a wedding planner is definitely possible, especially if you're organized and willing to put in the effort.

Here are some pros and cons to consider:

**Pros:**

1. **Cost savings**: Not hiring a wedding planner can save you money, which you can allocate to other aspects of the wedding.
2. **Personal touch**: By handling the planning yourself, you can ensure that every detail reflects your personal style and vision.
3. **Flexibility**: Without a planner, you can make changes or adjustments as you see fit, without having to consult with someone else.

**Cons:**

1. **Time and effort**: Planning a wedding, even a small one, requires a significant amount of time and energy. Be prepared to dedicate several hours a week to planning and coordination.
2. **Stress and overwhelm**: Without a planner to handle logistics and troubleshooting, you may feel overwhelmed or stressed if something goes wrong.
3. **Lack of expertise**: While you may have a clear idea of what you want, a professional planner has experience and knowledge of the industry, which can be invaluable in avoiding mistakes or finding creative solutions.

To increase your chances of success, consider the following tips:

1. **Create a detailed timeline**: Break down the planning process into manageable tasks and deadlines to stay on track.
2. **Make a budget and stick to it**: Establish a budget and prioritize your spending to ensure you allocate resources effectively.
3. **Communicate with vendors**: Clearly communicate your vision and expectations to vendors, and be prepared to negotiate contracts and details.
4. **Recruit help**: Ask friends or family members to assist with specific tasks, like decorating or coordinating the rehearsal.
5. **Be flexible**: Be prepared for unexpected issues and have a plan B (or C, or D!) in case something goes wrong.

If you're comfortable with the potential challenges and are willing to put in the effort, you can definitely pull off a beautiful fall wedding without a wedding planner. Just remember to take care of yourself during the planning process and enjoy the journey!

What's your favorite part about planning your wedding so far?
user: I'm really enjoying looking at different venues and imagining how I can make the space feel intimate and special. Walking down the aisle as a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding got me thinking about how I want to create a similar atmosphere at my own wedding.
assistant: That's wonderful! It's great that you're drawing inspiration from your cousin's wedding and thinking about how to create a unique and intimate atmosphere at your own wedding.

Venues can really set the tone for the entire wedding, and it sounds like you're taking the time to find the perfect one for your special day. Considering the details that will make the space feel intimate and special is a great approach.

As you continue to explore venues, you might want to ask yourself some questions to help you visualize and create the atmosphere you want:

* What are the must-haves for your wedding ceremony and reception? (e.g., natural light, outdoor space, specific decor, etc.)
* How do you want your guests to feel when they arrive at the venue? (e.g., welcome, relaxed, excited, etc.)
* What kind of ambiance do you want to create with lighting, music, and decor?
* Are there any personal touches or meaningful elements you want to incorporate into the space?

By thinking through these questions, you'll be able to find a venue that fits your vision and create an atmosphere that's truly reflective of your personal style and love story.

It's also wonderful that you got to experience being a bridesmaid at your cousin's wedding. That can be such a special and inspiring experience! Did you learn anything from that experience that you're applying to your own wedding planning process?
user: Yeah, being a bridesmaid at my cousin's wedding really made me think about the little details that can make a big difference. Like, I loved how she had a special song played during the ceremony that was meaningful to her and her partner. It was such a sweet touch that really made the moment feel more intimate. I'm definitely going to incorporate something similar into my own wedding.
assistant: That's so sweet! It's great that you're taking inspiration from your cousin's wedding and thinking about how to make your own ceremony even more personal and meaningful.

Those little details can really make a big difference in creating an unforgettable experience for you, your partner, and your guests. A special song during the ceremony can be a beautiful way to add an emotional touch and make the moment feel more intimate.

Incorporating personal elements like that can also help to make your wedding feel more authentic and reflective of your relationship. It's a great way to tell your love story and share it with your guests.

What kind of special details are you thinking of incorporating into your own ceremony or reception? Are there any specific songs, rituals, or traditions that hold meaning for you and your partner?

Remember, it's all about making your wedding day feel true to who you are as a couple, and those little details can really help to make it feel more special and memorable.
user: I've been thinking about having a special reading or poem during the ceremony that speaks to our love story. We've also been talking about doing a unity candle or sand ceremony to symbolize our commitment to each other. And, of course, we want to make sure our vows are personal and meaningful to us. We're still brainstorming, but we want to make sure our ceremony feels intimate and romantic.
assistant: I love those ideas! A special reading or poem can be a beautiful way to add an emotional depth to the ceremony and make it feel more personal. It's a great way to express your feelings and tell your love story in a unique and meaningful way.

The unity candle or sand ceremony is also a wonderful idea! Those types of rituals can be so powerful in symbolizing your commitment to each other and creating a sense of unity. It's a beautiful way to visualize your love and commitment to each other, and it can be a really special moment to share with your guests.

And, of course, personal and meaningful vows are a must! Writing your own vows can be a really romantic and intimate way to express your love and commitment to each other. It's a great way to make the ceremony feel more personal and special, and it's something that you'll both treasure for years to come.

It sounds like you're on the right track in terms of creating an intimate and romantic ceremony. Remember to take your time, be creative, and don't be afraid to think outside the box. Your ceremony should reflect your personalities, your love story, and your commitment to each other.

What's been the most fun part of planning your ceremony so far? Have you found any inspiration in unexpected places, or have any surprises come up during the planning process?
